text: robotic Mackey 1, thoracoscopic Mackey 1 as well as thoracoscopic Iver-Lewis oesophagectomies.
Two 10 mm ports are put in posterior axillary line in 5th and 9th intercostal space.
Another two 5 mm ports are kept in mid axillary line in 3rd and 7th intercostal space.
This is our standard marking.
As we can see here
C is the camera pod, W1 and W2 are our working pod and A is the assistant pod.
Entry dissection, dissection along the bronchus, posterior dissection, right recurrent laryngeal and left recurrent laryngeal lymph node dissection.
We are believers of entry first approach.
Our dissection starts at the inferior pulmonary ligament.
We retract the lung anteriorly and we start cutting inferior pulmonary ligament.
Aim for this step is to take all the fibro fatty tissue along with the lymph node with the specimen and define the inferior pulmonary vein.
Inferior pulmonary vein is the first structure to be defined in this surgery.
As we can see here, these all tiny lymph nodes are part of specimen.
Traction and counter traction are of paramount importance.
As we can see here, our assistant is giving traction by pulling the lung and surgeon himself is giving counter traction by retracting the specimen posteriorly.
There is a nice avascular plane always present between the inferior pulmonary vein and the specimen and all the fibrophytic tissue is part of that specimen.
Here we are taking all tissue with the specimen away from the inferior pulmonary vein and pericardium.
Good thing about this entry dissection is that along the pericardium we have very nice avascular plane stretching from hiatus right up to the subcarinal lymph node basin.
It also guide us right up to the opposite pleura.
Sharp dissection is the key here.
Our preferred instrument is monopolar hook as it is one of the precision instrument and it helps us in keeping our surgical field extremely dry.
We need to understand in the esophagectomy that when we are doing it for cancer, we have to take out all the lymph node as an end block along with the specimen.
There is no point of taking out them separately as long as possible.
There is a nice concept of mesoesophageal accession where anteriorly you take all tissue with the specimen

leaving behind all the important structure as a landmark which will guide you for your dissection similarly you proceed posteriorly you take all the fibrophytic tissue with the specimen in that way you can maintain the very good plane here we can see that we are going

along the pericardium right up to the hiatus we are taking everything with the specimen there is one important nodal basin situated here above the hiatus which is station 111 and it is one of the most commonly left behind lymph node as there are very high possibility that we may not address here if there is inferior vena cava prominently coming in our way
Here we are going towards the contralateral pleura and we will be able to see contralateral pleura in a moment.
This is the contralateral pleura appearing right here.
That is the limit of our contralateral dissection.
One important structure to be safeguarded here is inferior pulmonary vein.
There are two important basins situated above and below the inferior pulmonary vein on the left side which we are taking.
Now pleura over the right bronchus is cut and that area is exposed for further dissection.
It is very important to keep the field dry so that very tiny and important structure in this area are very well visualized.
This is the right bronchus and we are taking lymph nodes which are station 108 right away from the right bronchus and with the specimen.
Here important role of the assistant is to retract the right bronchus downward anteriorly and counter traction is given by the surgeon by lifting tissue posteriorly.
If we give right kind of counter-attraction, planes will open up automatically.
We can appreciate the nice plane of dissection.
Over the right bronchus, we need to define two important structures.
One is vagus nerve to the bronchus which is a proprioceptive nerve and has to be preserved for better post-operative recovery.
As we can see here we are selectively cutting the vagus now going into the esophagus and preserving its bronchial branches.
Another important structure is right bronchial artery.
As we can see here right bronchial artery is nicely visualized and we are selectively cutting the branches of it going into the esophagus.

when we are separating esophagus from the trachea we need to understand that there are no fat in between them and we need to stay flush to the esophagus and dissecting parallel to them here we are able to appreciate the left side of the border of trachea which is an important landmark which will guide us for the dissection next step is to define
and delineate the left side of the bronchus which is nicely visible here.
Once dissection along the tracheobronchial tree is completed thoroughly we switch to posterior dissection.
In posterior dissection we stay flush to the ozygous vein and put a posterior cut on the mediastinal pleura.
Our aim here is to take all the fibrophytic tissue

away from the ozygous vein and define the prevascular fascia one important anatomical consideration here is that prevascular fascia covers the descending aorta and fat outside the prevascular fascia contains thoracic duct so whenever we are planning thoracic duct clipping it is very useful anatomical landmark for
confident clipping of thoracic duct and its subsidiary branches.
As we can see here, we are dissecting the prevascular fascia and opening it.
Descending aorta is peeping out from the prevascular fascia and all the fat containing thoracic duct is lifted away from the

descending outer and azagus vein at this point we will clip the thoracic duct and cut it once that is done we proceed with our posterior dissection in which everything is taken away from the descending outer we go right up to the contralateral pleura as we have done

anteriorly here station 112 lymph node is taken with the specimen there are usually very nice plane of separation between mesoesophagus and the left side of the pleura it is better not to open pleura at this phase but if we accidentally open the left side of the pleura then at the end of our thoracoscopic phase we enlarge the window of that opening so that we don't need to put the
contralateral intercostal drainage tube.
Once infra-axiacus dissection is completed we switch to supra-axiacus dissection which is the most important part of our surgery.
First we do right side of the

recurrent laryngeal lymph node dissection first step for this dissection is define the superior vena cava and put a pleural cut along the superior vena cava reach right up to the apex of the chest and define the right subclavian artery
We all know that right recurrent laryngeal lymph node encircles the right subclavian artery.
So it is extremely important to go step by step, define all important structure and automatically we will be able to see the right recurrent laryngeal now.
Here we can see vagus nerve, subclavian artery and right recurrent laryngeal nerve arising from the vagus nerve and encircling the subclavian artery.
Once that is done, we know where is our right recurrent laryngeal limb.
Now we start separating the specimen from the trachea and go towards the neck.

at this juncture it is very important to understand that when we are using harmonic for this dissection we need to keep active blade of the harmonic away from the membranous portion of the trachea or it will accidentally injure the trachea as we can see here whenever i am applying the energy i am tilting my active blade away from the membranous portion of the trachea
Once that is done, we start dissecting along the right side of the vagus nerve.
Here we can nicely see the right side of the vagus nerve coming across the trachea and going towards the right subclavian artery.
In between the fork made by trachea and the vagus nerve lies the station 106 tracheobronchial lymph node.

that lymph node is a part of our specimen here we can see the right recurrent laryngeal lymph now nicely and we are taking all fibrophytic tissue with right recurrent laryngeal lymph node group and mass

sharp dissection is very important here we have to make sure that field remains dry then and then we will be able to see everything clearly we have to take a pause in between so that we give time to the active blade to cool down otherwise there will be thermal injury to the now
Scissor is also one of the important tool to use at this juncture to define and delineate the fibers of the nerve.
There are fibers of the nerve going into the specimen which we have to cut so that we can define the main trunk of the right recurrent nerve.
Here we are seeing the right recurrent laryngeal nerve nicely and posteriorly situated lymph nodes are part of our right lymph node packet.
Combination of energy source and scissor is very useful in this area.
we have to remain extremely patient while dissecting in this area and vigilant for any anomalous branches.
Sometimes recurrent laryngeal nerve rise 1 or 2 cm above
subclavian artery go along the vigorous now and then encircles the subclavian artery so that is one important thing we need to realize when we are defining the right recurrent laryngeal now
Upper limit of the right side recurrent laryngeal lymph node dissection is inferior thyroid artery.
Many a times we see the esophageal branches of the inferior thyroid artery and that is the limit where we need to stop.
There is no point of berry picking this lymph node.
Oncologically it is always safe to take out entire lymph node packet and mass as a one specimen.

here we are seeing that entire specimen is coming en masse with station 106 recurrent lymph node that finishes the right recurrent dissection then we switch to the left side recurrent dissection first step here is
to separate the specimen from the left side of the trachea.
Here one important maneuver is tracheal dip where we go along the left side of the trachea and we take all fibro fatty tissue away from the trachea.
One important fascia to be noted is visceral fascia which covers the left recurrent laryngeal lymph node and the

now once we take everything away from the trachea then lymph node and the now is lifted with the specimen and that makes our further dissection very easy here we can see the left recurrent laryngeal now and now we are gradually separating the AP window lymph node

from the trachea and from the MPA which is main pulmonary artery in the floor one important thing to note about the AP window lymph node is that their arrangement they are arranged in a single file pattern so that there are very high possibility that once we take out one lymph node there will be chain of another lymph node from its behind so we have to be diligent in taking out all of them

gradually one by one for the suprazygous lymph node dissection it is very important to have a trained cameraman who shows us camera because if we have untrained camera person then we will have a lot of struggle especially while doing these steps of the surgery and there will be a lot of difficulties as well as risk associated with the procedure
Here we can see descending aorta posteriorly, trachea anteriorly and cleared AP window.
These are the lymph node attached with the specimen, left recurrent laryngeal now, right recurrent laryngeal now, at the end of the surgery.
This is the final picture at the end of the closer.
I would also like to show you how we perform Intrathoracic Minimal Invasive Iverlewis with Intrathoracic Anastomosis.
All the steps are similar to McEwan Esophagectomy but at the end of that we
Prepare the esophagus for the anastomosis by separating the lymph nodes from it.
Then we pull the specimen from the abdomen.
As we know that in Ivor Lewis first step is abdominal phase and once abdominal phase is done then we start our thoracic phase.
Port placement and position of the patient is the exact same.
Everything done and then we cut at the appropriate place.
Esophagus transaction is completed.
We convert one of our seventh intercostal space pot in the utility incision.
We extract the specimen from that same utility pot.
We prepare the conduit by applying couple of staplers.
As we can see, we can take entire of the fundus with the specimen to achieve good castric margin.

upper end of the oesophagus is prepared for the anastomosis gastric conduit is thoroughly washed from inside and then side to side stapled anastomosis is performed for Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy here we can see that we are applying first endoGI stapling for side to side anastomosis
Important thing to note here is that we need at least 6-7 cm of the mobile oesophagus for sound tension free anastomosis.
Once that stapler is applied, we apply another couple of staplers to finish our entrotomy.
In Iverlewis oesophagectomy, apart from ICD, we also put
negative suction drain to drain the conduit this is the final picture at the end of surgery thank
